1:
The chapter opens with the Ziphites' second betrayal of David, and the witnesses read the scene on three levels — its geography, its motive, and, in one expositor, the harder question of whether the whole episode is a duplicate of what went before. The grammatical-historical commentators fix the setting. Keil and Delitzsch note that this second treachery falls after David's marriage to Abigail, once he had returned to the desert of Judah, and that the hill of Hachilah 'before Jeshimon' is the very ground named at the first betrayal (1 Samuel 23:19); 'before the face of' the wilderness, they add, is defined more exactly there as 'to the south of' it. Clarke simply marks it 'the second time that these enemies of David endeavoured to throw him into the hands of Saul.'

Charles Ellicott raises the one real debate. Is this merely another account of the sparing told in the cave (1 Samuel 24)? He records that Ewald and the Bishop of Bath and Wells take the two narratives as one incident differently told, while Keil, Erdmann, Lange, Payne Smith and Wordsworth hold them to be two distinct events. Weighing the details — the midday siesta in the En-gedi cavern against the night raid on a sleeping camp — Ellicott concludes 'with some certainty' that they are separate occasions. He then presses the graver question the compiler leaves unanswered: why insert a second narrative of so similar a kind in a book where conciseness is 'ever so carefully studied'? His only answer is that everything conducing to the glory of Israel's favourite hero, and above all 'the surpassing nobility and generosity' of David's magnanimity to his deadly foe, was deemed worthy of a second detailed telling.

The reformed Gill and the puritan Matthew Henry turn to the men's motive and to Saul's relapse. Gill suspects the Ziphites acted of themselves rather than at Saul's prompting, driven by fear that David, once come to the throne, would remember their treachery, and so eager to have him 'cut off by the hand of Saul.' Henry adds the sadder note about the king: Saul had lately been in 'the same good mind' he showed at the cave (1 Samuel 24:17), and might have troubled David no more had not the Ziphites put him on. From this Henry draws his pastoral warning — that since we carry 'so much of the tinder of corruption in our own hearts,' we must pray that the sparks of temptation be kept far from us, 'lest, if they come together, we be set on fire of hell.' The witnesses agree that a good impression unwatched is soon lost, and the reconciled Saul relapses at the first malicious whisper.
2:
Saul takes the field with three thousand chosen men, and the witnesses read the verse for the king's unchanged heart and the calculated strength of his pursuit. The reformed Gill hears the swiftness of relapse: Saul 'immediately' arose, 'glad of an opportunity to seize on David, having the same disposition towards him as ever,' and perhaps regretting that he had not laid hold of him when David followed him out of the cave. The good mind of the previous chapter has not survived the first fresh provocation.

The grammatical-historical commentators attend to the numbers and the logistics. Clarke observes that although Saul knew David commanded only six hundred, he 'thought it was not safe to pursue such an able general with a less force,' and that the three thousand were 'elect or picked men' chosen so that he might the better depend upon them. Ellicott identifies these as the standing army Saul kept about him at Gibeah, first noticed at 1 Samuel 13:2. He also fills in the human background: after the marriage to Abigail, David and his now considerable band returned to their old neighbourhood in the south, and David, 'after the protestation of Saul at En-gedi,' probably supposed he would at last be left in peace. But he 'forgot with whom he had to do' — forgot how readily 'the old mania would return with redoubled force' — and the Ziphites, who knew both the country and the mind of Saul's court, simply repeated their old tactics. The temptation to resume the hunt was, Ellicott says, 'too great to be resisted,' and Saul, likely with Abner's counsel, marched.

Together the witnesses draw a single moral portrait. The size and quality of the force measure not David's danger only but the depth of Saul's obsession: a king who will muster his crack troops against a fugitive of six hundred has let a private hatred swallow his judgment. Gill's 'same disposition as ever' and Ellicott's 'redoubled force' say the same thing from opposite sides — that convictions which do not go down to a renewed heart leave a man exactly where they found him, and that the reconciliations of an unstable soul are only truces. The very thoroughness of the expedition, three thousand chosen men after one man, prepares the reader to marvel the more when that whole army is found, in a few verses, helpless and asleep.
3:
The armies take up their positions, and the witnesses use this short verse to mark how differently the second pursuit unfolds from the first. The reformed Gill notes only that Saul pitched 'in the hill of Hachilah, which is before Jeshimon, by the way' — 'the very same place where the Ziphites suggested David was' — so that the trap is laid precisely where the informers had pointed.

The weight of the comment falls to the grammatical-historical Ellicott, who draws out the contrast with the earlier flight. On the former occasion, when the Ziphites first brought Saul to Hachilah, David heard of the march and 'retired into the wilderness of Maon'; Saul pursued, and David's band was saved from destruction only by the news of a Philistine invasion that called the royal forces away, after which David withdrew unmolested and took refuge at En-gedi (1 Samuel 23). Now, Ellicott observes, the pattern is broken: 'David does not flee in haste to Maon, and thence to En-gedi, nor is Saul called away to any Philistine invasion; but David abides in the wilderness,' and his scouts come and tell him that Saul 'in very deed' (v.4) was come after him in force.

The single point the two make together is that David has changed his posture. He no longer runs; he stays, watches, and prepares to act. Where the first betrayal set him fleeing from ridge to ridge, this one finds him settled enough to send out spies and verify the report with his own eyes before he stirs. The witnesses do not moralize the verse heavily, but the implied lesson is plain: David has learned the measure of his enemy. He does not doubt that Saul, for all his tears at the cave, is capable of coming again — indeed he will not believe it 'till he had the utmost evidence of it,' as Henry says of the next verse — yet neither does he panic. The steady abiding in the wilderness, over against the earlier headlong retreat, is the first sign of the self-possession that will carry David, unarmed of malice, straight into the sleeping camp of the man who has come to kill him. The geography that once meant escape now becomes the theatre of a second, greater proof of his loyalty.
4:
David sends out spies and learns that Saul has indeed come, and the two witnesses who treat the verse make it a small lesson in the marriage of faith and prudence. The reformed Gill states the principle plainly: David sent scouts 'to observe if he was coming or come, and where he was, that he might not be surprised by him; for though David knew the Lord was and would be his protection, he thought proper to be upon his guard, and to make use of means for his safety.' The confidence that God will keep him does not make David careless; it makes him watchful, for the God who promised him the kingdom is the same God who appoints the means by which he is preserved to it.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch add the philological and practical detail. The Hebrew phrase behind 'in very deed' (rendered 'for a certainty') is the same construction used at 1 Samuel 23:23: David does not act on rumour but waits until the report is confirmed beyond doubt. Only then, they note, does he rise 'with two attendants to reconnoitre the camp of Saul' — the companions named in the next verse. The care is deliberate and layered: first the spies, then David's own eyes upon the camp before ever he ventures in.

Between them the witnesses sketch the temper of the man. David will not believe the worst of Saul on hearsay, as Henry remarks elsewhere — he 'would not believe that Saul would deal so basely with him till he had the utmost evidence of it' — and this reluctance is itself a kind of charity. Yet once the certainty is in hand he acts without delay and without recklessness. The verse thus quietly rebukes two opposite errors: the presumption that trusts God while neglecting ordinary caution, and the fear that panics before the danger is even proved. David holds both together, sending the means ahead of the venture and reserving his judgment of Saul until the evidence is sure. The spies are not a failure of faith but its ordinary instrument; the God who will not let his hand fall upon the Lord's anointed is trusted, here, to work through watchfulness rather than to excuse it.
5:
David comes to Saul's camp and beholds the king lying within the entrenchment, Abner beside him and the people round about. The witnesses concentrate on one obscure word — the 'trench' in which Saul lay — and the grammatical-historical commentators work out its sense. Clarke reports the range of readings for the Hebrew bammaegal: 'in the trench,' 'in the midst of his carriages,' 'in a ring of carriages,' 'in the circle' formed by the troops; since the root signifies anything round, he inclines with Luther's wagenburg to a rampart or fortress made of wagons, and notes the Arab custom of encamping in a circle 'and putting their principal officers in the centre.' Ellicott agrees: the king 'lay down within the barricade or rampart formed by the baggage wagons,' Keil's 'wagon rampart,' the Septuagint's 'covered chariots.' The picture is exact — Saul in the protected heart of the encampment, his general at his side, the fighting men ringed about him.

The reformed Gill adds only that David 'came near it, within sight of it,' near enough to take the view with his naked eye and mark how Saul was encamped — the quiet, patient observation that Henry places 'probably in the dusk of the evening,' David undiscovered, studying the entrenchment before he moves.

The wesleyan Wesley lifts the reader's eye back to the human cause of the whole scene. He fastens not on the camp but on the informers: 'the Ziphites — probably Saul would have pursued David no more, had not these wretches set him on.' It is the same judgment Henry passed on the first verse, and it frames the sleeping camp with a note of pathos and of blame. All this military array, three thousand men entrenched behind their wagons with the king safe at the centre, has been set in motion by a piece of malice; and the very completeness of Saul's defences — the ring of carriages, the ring of soldiers — is about to be exposed as no defence at all. The witnesses leave the irony to speak for itself: the more elaborately the camp is fortified, the more astonishing it will be that David walks into its centre and out again unresisted.
6:
David asks who will go down with him into the camp, and Abishai volunteers. The grammatical-historical commentators spend themselves on the two companions. Keil and Delitzsch introduce Ahimelech the Hittite and Abishai the son of Zeruiah, David's sister (1 Chronicles 2:16) and brother of Joab, 'afterwards a celebrated general of David' (2 Samuel). Ellicott expands both. The Hittites were an old Canaanite people, subdued but not exterminated (Genesis 15:20), living in a kind of joint occupation of the land; Ahimelech here ranks among David's 'trusted faithful men,' as another Hittite, Uriah, would later, though we hear no more of him. Of Abishai and his brothers Ellicott tells the darker story: between these sons of Zeruiah and Abner 'a blood feud seems to have existed,' which explains David's bitter words to Abner in this very chapter and foreshadows the murder of Abner in cold blood (2 Samuel 3). Abishai, he says, 'with the reckless gallantry and the intense devotion to David' that marked these warrior kinsmen, 'at once volunteered to go with his loved chief,' while Ahimelech 'seems to have backed out of the perilous night enterprise.'

The puritan Matthew Henry reads the moment for its courage and its source. David 'proposed it to him and to another of his confidants,' but the other declined, or at least let Abishai run the risk. Whether David 'was prompted to this by his own courage, or by an extraordinary impression upon his spirits, or by the oracle, does not appear'; but, Henry says, 'like Gideon, he ventured through the guards, with a special assurance of the divine protection.' The reformed Gill notes only that David 'addressed himself to the two following persons.'

The witnesses do not divide; they build one portrait of a venture that is at once audacious and guarded. David does not go alone into three thousand men, nor does he take a crowd; he takes one companion, and that the boldest. The Gideon parallel Henry supplies is the interpretive key the others imply — this is no rash bravado but a warranted daring, a man moving under the conviction that God will keep him. And the quiet aside about Abishai's temper, the reckless devotion that will one day spill Abner's blood, sets David's coming restraint in sharp relief: the same night will show the servant eager to kill and the master forbidding it.
7:
David and Abishai reach the sleeping camp by night, and find Saul asleep within the trench, his spear thrust into the ground at his head. The witnesses divide their attention between the daring of the deed, the emblem of the spear, and the conviction that God has arranged it all. The reformed Gill first corrects Josephus, who 'wrongly says that he took with him both Abishai and Ahimelech'; David 'chose to have but one, for the greater secrecy.' Then Gill marvels at the boldness — 'for two men to go into a camp of three thousand men' — and grounds it in more than nerve: David 'was moved to this, not merely by the dint of his natural courage, but by the Spirit of God,' assured that Saul and the people had been cast into a deep sleep by the Lord, and moved to it so that he might have 'a second time' the chance to convince Saul of his innocence.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott dwells on the spear at Saul's head. 'Bolster,' he explains, is literally 'the place where his head is,' better rendered 'at his head'; the same Hebrew word describes Jacob's stones at Bethel (Genesis 28:11). The spear itself is the recurring sign of the king — 'in battle in his hand, and in the council chamber and at the state banquet within his reach,' and now 'reared upright beside the sleeping king.' He quotes Thomson's Land and the Book: at every encampment the sheik's tent was marked 'by a tall spear stuck upright in the ground in front of it,' the very custom that here designates where Saul lies.

The wesleyan Wesley states most fully the providence Gill only touches. That David 'came... to Saul's host' 'might seem a bold and strange attempt,' but three things warrant it: David 'had a particular assurance that God would preserve him to the kingdom'; he 'had a special instinct from God to this work'; and 'possibly God might inform him that he had cast them into a deep sleep, that he might have this second opportunity of manifesting his innocency towards Saul.' The witnesses converge on a single reading: this is not recklessness but obedience. The spear that stands for royal power stands helpless beside its sleeping owner, and David, led by the Spirit and shielded by a God-sent slumber, walks into the heart of the camp not to seize the crown but to prove his innocence.
8:
Abishai, seeing the king asleep and the spear within reach, begs leave to strike: 'God hath delivered thine enemy into thine hand this day; now therefore let me smite him.' The witnesses read the moment as the exact echo of an earlier temptation and as a study in the difference between opportunity and warrant. The wesleyan Clarke marks the repetition precisely: 'Here Abishai uses the same language as did David's men, when Saul came into the cave at En-gedi' (1 Samuel 24), 'and David uses the same language in reply.' The providence that lays the enemy bare is read, once again, as if it were a command to kill — the same inference David had already refused once, and will refuse again.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott catches the savagery of Abishai's eagerness. The words 'I will not smite him the second time' mean, in Lange's paraphrase, 'I will pin him to the ground so thoroughly with one blow that it will not need another to kill him' — the Vulgate's blunt et secundo non opus erit, 'there will be no need of a second.' There is no cruelty of intention in Abishai beyond a soldier's directness, but the phrase leaves no doubt of what he proposes: one spear-thrust, final and sufficient. The reformed Gill sets the scene simply — Abishai spoke 'seeing Saul fast asleep, and a spear so near him,' the weapon and the opportunity together prompting the thought.

Beneath the brief exchange the witnesses find the chapter's central tension. To Abishai the case is closed: the enemy is here, God has handed him over, one blow ends the long persecution and opens David's way to the throne. Every circumstance seems to argue for it — the sleeping guards, the spear at hand, the sheer improbability of such an opening occurring by chance. And precisely here the witnesses locate the test of David's faith. That God has permitted the opportunity is plain; that God has commanded the deed does not follow. Abishai reads providence as licence; David, in the next verse, will read the same providence and reach the opposite conclusion, because he measures the moment not by his advantage but by the sanctity of the one whom God has anointed. The temptation is at its strongest just when it wears the face of divine gift.
9:
David forbids the blow with a principle rather than a preference: 'Destroy him not; for who can stretch forth his hand against the Lord's anointed, and be guiltless?' The witnesses agree that everything turns on the sanctity of the anointing, and the grammatical-historical Ellicott gives the doctrine its fullest statement. David, 'taught, no doubt, by the prophet Samuel, looked upon the person of Saul as made sacred and inviolable by the royal anointing.' 'Through the anointing Saul had become the possession of Jehovah; only Jehovah, then, could lawfully take away that sacred life.' Ellicott adds the shrewd historical guess that these 'exalted sentiments respecting the Divine rights of kings' may not have been spoken there in the dark among Saul's soldiers, but rehearsed afterward as David and Abishai talked the night over.

The wesleyan Wesley presses the same point onto David's own conscience and station: 'Though Saul be a tyrant, yet he is our lord and king; and I, though designed king, as yet am his subject; and therefore cannot kill him without sin, nor will I consent that thou shouldst do it.' The refusal is double — David will neither strike nor permit the striking — and it rests not on Saul's deserts, which are nothing, but on David's own place under God's ordering. The reformed Gill notes simply that David 'laid his commands upon' Abishai 'not to hurt him,' and Keil and Delitzsch let the verse run straight on into the three alternatives of the next.

The thing the witnesses underline together is where David locates the danger. His concern, as Henry observes of this passage, 'was guilt, and his concern respected his innocence more than his safety.' Abishai had weighed the opportunity; David weighs the sin. To lift a hand against the Lord's anointed is to 'resist the ordinance of God' (Romans 13:2), and 'no man could do it and be guiltless.' The crown that God has promised David he will not snatch by a crime against the crown God has not yet removed. Here the witnesses find the moral centre of the chapter: a man so certain of his future kingdom that he can afford to spare the king who is hunting him, because he trusts the God who anoints to be also the God who deposes.
10:
David presses his refusal further by naming the ways Saul might die — 'the Lord shall smite him; or his day shall come to die; or he shall descend into battle, and perish' — and the witnesses read the verse as David deliberately handing vengeance back to God. The wesleyan Clarke lays out the three cases as 'the three ordinary ways by which man accomplishes... his day': Saul may 'die by a stroke of the Divine judgment,' or reach a 'natural death, which in the course of things must be before mine,' or 'fall by the enemies of his country.' Whichever it be, Clarke concludes for David, 'murder David could not consider to be lawful; this would have been taking the matter out of God's hand, and this David would not do.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott reads the same three possibilities and notices what lies behind the first of them. David 'suggests three possible cases in which the Divine arm might smite the anointed of Jehovah': a sudden death from disease — 'no doubt, the recent death of Nabal was in his mind' (1 Samuel 25) — then a natural death, and then 'some blow received in battle,' with 'the idea of an arrow winged by some unseen hand' in the speaker's thoughts. The reformed Gill sees the pastoral aim of the speech: David spoke thus 'to make Abishai easy, and prevent his doing what he proposed' — the argument is meant to quiet the eager kinsman as much as to justify the king's sparing.

The witnesses take the verse as the positive side of David's principle. It is not enough to say he will not kill Saul; he must show why he can afford not to. And the answer is that God has more than one way to accomplish what David is forbidden to do by his own hand. The fresh memory of Nabal, struck down within the fortnight by no human blade, is the standing proof that vengeance may safely be left with God. David is content to 'wait till God shall think fit to avenge him,' as Henry puts it, 'rather than force my way to the promised crown by any indirect methods.' The verse thus binds patience to providence: because the Lord has so many arrows, David needs none of his own, and the promised throne can be received as a gift rather than seized as a prize.
11:
David seals his refusal with an oath — 'the Lord forbid that I should stretch forth mine hand against the Lord's anointed' — and directs Abishai instead to take the spear and the cruse of water. The reformed Gill hears the vehemence: David speaks of the very thought 'with the utmost detestation and abhorrence.' What Abishai urged as a duty David recoils from as a sacrilege.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott makes the two objects David carries off carry a weight of meaning. The spear 'was Saul's especial sign of royalty,' and its removal is read as a portent: 'this taking away of the spear from Saul's head as he slept was an omen of the transfer of his royalty to David' — Bishop Patrick's words, cited by Wordsworth. The cruse of water Ellicott dignifies by an ancient custom Ewald describes: 'some high dignitary always had in keeping a costly ewer for the king's necessary ablutions,' whose disappearance 'would involve almost as great a disgrace to the king as the loss of his sceptre.' Ewald sets the incident among 'many other instances of similar stories, in which the future conqueror and founder of a new dynasty is represented as having received at first some symbol of royalty from his predecessors as if by accident, or in sport' — as Alexander took the royal divining cup from Darius — 'and in nothing was the belief in omens so strong as in the high affairs of state.'

The witnesses thus turn the spear and the cruse into a silent argument. David will not take Saul's life, but he will take the tokens of Saul's life and royalty, and these become at once his proof and his prophecy. They are proof, because to hold Saul's own spear is to show past dispute how completely the king had been at his mercy; and they are prophecy, because the emblems of kingship pass, unbidden and unbloodied, from the sleeping Saul into the hand of the man God has anointed to succeed him. Where Abishai would have transferred the kingdom by a spear-thrust, David receives its foreshadowing by a spear carried gently away. The oath and the trophies belong together: the hand that refuses to strike is the hand into which the royalty is quietly given.
12:
David takes the spear and the cruse from beside Saul's head, and the whole camp sleeps on: 'no man saw it, nor knew it, neither awaked; for they were all asleep, because a deep sleep from the Lord was fallen upon them.' The reformed Gill first explains why David, not Abishai, carried the trophies: either Abishai 'refused to take them, since he might not take away his life,' or, more likely, David 'took them himself, lest Abishai should be tempted, when so near to Saul, and his spear in his hand, and should thrust him with it.' Even the removal of the tokens is governed by David's care to keep his companion from the very sin he has forbidden.

The grammatical-historical commentators press the strange slumber. Clarke notes that 'a deep sleep from the Lord' uses 'the same word which is used' of the sleep God caused to fall on Adam when he formed Eve (Genesis 2:21), and adds the realistic touch that each Arab warrior keeps his lance stuck in the ground beside him and his cruse of water at hand like a soldier's canteen. Ellicott makes the Hebrew more graphic — 'and none saw, and none knew, and none awaked' — and then weighs the cause. 'At first sight' the inference 'certainly is that an unnatural, or rather a supernatural drowsiness had fallen on the camp'; yet, he cautions, 'it is not absolutely necessary to suppose that a special miracle was wrought.' The men were 'in a friendly district, and never dreamed of a surprise,' 'possibly the rough soldiers on duty had been carousing,' and David and Abishai were 'practised scouts' who 'did their perilous work speedily and noiselessly.'

Here the witnesses genuinely divide. The larger body — Gill, Clarke, and Henry — take the sleep as an act of God, 'a deep sleep from the Lord,' so heavy that 'David and Abishai walked and talked among them, and yet none of them stirred'; Henry marvels that 'how easily can God weaken the strongest, befool the wisest, and baffle the most watchful,' hearing behind it the psalm's 'they have slept their sleep' (Psalms 76:5). Ellicott alone leaves room for a natural explanation, the very carelessness David is about to mock in Abner. Yet even he does not deny the providence, only its miraculous manner; and the point on which all agree is the same — that Saul's whole defence, spear and guards and general, lay powerless while the Lord kept his anointed servant unseen. Our strength and comfort, Henry warns, are lost 'when we are careless, and secure, and off our watch.'
13:
David crosses to the far side of the valley and stands on a hilltop 'afar off, a great space being between them,' before he calls out. The witnesses read the distance as the deliberate mark of a man who no longer trusts Saul's nearness. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch make the point explicitly: 'David reckoned far less now upon any change in the state of Saul's mind than he had done before, when he followed Saul without hesitation from the cave and called after him' (1 Samuel 24); now 'he rather feared lest Saul should endeavour to get him into his power as soon as the deed was accomplished,' and so he keeps a 'deep steep ravine' between himself and the camp. Ellicott concurs and quotes Keil to the same effect: the mountain was 'afar off, not, as we should say, as the crow flies, but afar, because a deep steep ravine lay between.'

The reformed Gill fills in the topography from Josephus — David 'went over the passage,' the valley between the two hills, and 'perhaps a brook that ran in the valley,' before gaining 'the top of a mountain.' The wesleyan Wesley draws the practical reason for the distance: 'afar off, that his person might be out of their reach, and yet his voice might be heard, which in a clear air, and in the silence of the night, might be heard at a great distance.'

The puritan Matthew Henry gathers these into the shape of the scene that follows. David 'posts himself conveniently, so that they might hear him and yet not reach him,' and then 'begins to reason with them upon what had passed.' Henry notes the further proof of the miracle in this very arrangement: 'the hand of God locked them up in this deep sleep, that, as soon as ever David had got out of danger, a very little thing awakened them, even David's voice at a great distance roused them.' The witnesses do not divide. Together they show a David more cautious than at En-gedi — having learned that Saul's tears are no security — yet still bent not on flight but on speech. He has put a ravine between himself and the spears precisely so that he may safely say what the sparing has earned him the right to say, and the same God who guarded him in the camp now lets his single voice, carried across the gorge in the still night, wake and hold a whole army.
14:
David's first words are shouted not to Saul but to Abner: 'Answerest thou not, Abner?' The witnesses read the address as pointed and ironic, aimed at the man whose duty it was to guard the king. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch put the case plainly: 'David called out to Abner, whose duty it was as general to defend the life of his king,' and Abner's reply, 'Who art thou that criest to the king?', means — as Ellicott confirms from the Vulgate — 'Who art thou that criest and disquietest the king?', that is, 'disturbest the king's rest with your shouting.' The general's first instinct is not alarm at a breach of the camp but annoyance at the noise.

The wesleyan Wesley adds the small circumstantial note that David 'cried to the people' when 'it is probable this was early in the morning' — the grey of dawn, when the voice carries and the sleepers are only beginning to stir. It is the hour that will let Saul recognize the voice before he can make out the face.

The puritan Matthew Henry supplies the tone the others imply. David, he says, 'reasons ironically with Abner, and keenly banters him.' Though David knew well 'that it was from the mighty power of God that Abner and the rest of the guards were cast into so deep a sleep,' he 'reproaches Abner as unworthy to be captain of the lifeguards, since he could sleep when the king his master lay so much exposed.' Henry hears in Abner's easy rousing the confirmation of the miracle — the guards who could not be woken by two men walking among them are startled awake by one voice across a valley. The witnesses do not divide; they take the challenge to Abner as David's chosen way of making his point before Saul himself. By calling first upon the negligent general, David forces the whole camp to reckon with a fact it slept through: that the king's life had lain, all night, in the hand of the man they were hunting, and that the men sworn to defend it had done nothing at all. The banter is sharp, but its edge is turned to Saul's benefit — it will end by proving David the truest guardian Saul had.
15:
David turns the irony full upon Abner: 'Art not thou a valiant man? and who is like to thee in Israel? wherefore then hast thou not kept thy lord the king?' The grammatical-historical commentators catch both the sarcasm and the strategy. Keil and Delitzsch explain the sting: David 'taunted Abner with having watched the king carelessly, and made himself chargeable with his death,' for 'one of the people came to destroy thy lord the king'; and the spear and pitcher are produced as proof. These reproaches, they add, were 'intended to show to Saul, who might at any rate possibly hear, and in fact did hear, that David was the most faithful defender of his life, more faithful than his closest and most zealous servants.' Ellicott weighs the word rendered 'valiant man,' the Hebrew ish used in its 'nobler' sense, as when the 'noble ones' are set against the mean in Psalms 49:2 and 'mean men and great men' in Isaiah 2:9.

Ellicott also hears in 'the whole of this bitter sarcastic address' the sign of 'a deadly feud' between David and Abner — which makes David's later magnanimity the more striking, his friendship with Abner and 'his deep sorrow for the great captain's untimely death' (2 Samuel 3). The wesleyan Clarke calls it 'a strong irony' and explains the force of David's verdict, 'ye are worthy to die': the guards 'deserve death for this neglect,' and 'had not Saul been so deeply affected with David's generosity,' he might well 'have put Abner and his chief officers to death,' though in truth they were not to blame, 'their apparent neglect being the effect of a supernatural sleep.' The reformed Gill sums the man: Abner was 'of great fame for courage and valour,' holding 'the next post in the army under Saul,' but 'had not behaved like a man, worthy of his character and office.'

The witnesses read the taunt as a double-edged instrument. On its surface it shames the captain of the guard, exposing the gap between his reputation and his performance — 'who is like to thee in Israel,' and yet the king slept unwatched. But its real target is over Abner's head. By convicting the general of failing to keep Saul, David silently establishes that he himself had kept him: the man branded a traitor is the only one in the field who guarded the king's life when it lay open to the spear. The irony is the argument, and the missing spear is its evidence.
16:
David presses the verdict home: 'This thing is not good that thou hast done. As the Lord liveth, ye are worthy to die, because ye have not kept your master, the Lord's anointed.' The witnesses first mark the restraint of the phrasing. The reformed Gill observes that 'this thing is not good' is deliberate understatement — 'a great fault, and very blameworthy' — 'expressed in a figure called meiosis, in which less is said than is intended.' David's mildness of speech only sharpens the gravity of the charge: to lose the king would have been a capital failure, and he states it as though it were merely 'not good.'

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch let the sentence run on to its proof and its effect: 'ye are worthy to die, because ye have not kept your master, the Lord's anointed. And now see where the king's spear is, and the cruse of water that was at his bolster.' The evidence lies in Abner's own missing gear; and the verse ends by turning Saul's ear toward David, for 'Saul knew David's voice' in the words that follow.

The puritan Matthew Henry draws out the courtroom logic. The guards, he says, 'deserved to lose their heads by martial law, for being off their guard, when they had the king himself asleep in the midst of them.' Then he points to the exhibit: 'Ecce signum — behold this token. See where the king's spear is, in the hand of him whom the king himself is pleased to count his enemy. Those that took away this might as easily and safely have taken away his life.' The question David forces is one of loyalty inverted: 'Now see who are the king's best friends — you that neglected him and left him exposed, or I that protected him when he was exposed.' Henry adds the moral turn that gives the verse its edge: 'Sometimes those that unjustly condemn others are justly left to fall into condemnation themselves.' The men who had marched three thousand strong to hunt David as one 'worthy to die' are now, by their own negligence, the ones on whom that sentence properly falls. The witnesses converge on the irony without softening it: the spear held aloft across the valley is at once David's acquittal and the whole camp's indictment, and it silences the charge of treason by the simplest of proofs — the traitor is the only man who kept the king alive.
17:
Saul recognizes the voice and answers with the old tenderness: 'Is this thy voice, my son David?' The witnesses attend to the naturalness of the scene and to the pathos of the greeting. The reformed Gill marks the difference between the general and the king: 'Abner at first did not' know the voice, 'as appears by his words, but Saul did, by being by this time thoroughly awake through the discourse that passed between David and Abner.' The exchange with the captain has served, among other things, to rouse the king fully so that he can hear his accuser and his preserver speak.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott praises the verse's realism: 'The account is most natural throughout.' The enterprise had been undertaken 'by night,' when the soldiers had fallen into a deep sleep; and when David 'stood on the opposite ridge, it was still, no doubt, the dawn of early morning.' So Saul 'speaks of hearing that voice of David so well known to him, and which once he so dearly loved'; in the dim light 'he could not as yet discern the figure of his former friend.' The voice reaches him before the face — and it is a voice charged with memory.

The puritan Matthew Henry lingers on the word 'my son.' Saul answers David 'in the same manner he had expressed his relentings' at the cave (1 Samuel 24). Henry marvels at the contradiction: 'He had given his wife to another, and yet calls him son; thirsted after his blood, and yet is glad to hear his voice.' And he draws a sober qualification: 'Those are bad indeed that have never any convictions of good, nor ever sincerely utter good expressions' — Saul's relentings are real as feeling even where they are false as resolve. Henry notes too that David now 'has as fair an opportunity of reaching Saul's conscience as he had just now of taking away his life,' and lays hold of the one rather than the other. The witnesses do not divide. They see in 'my son David' the genuine but unstable stirring of a heart that can be moved and yet will not be changed — melted for a night, hardened by the morning — and they set David's steady appeal against Saul's flickering affection as the true measure of the two men.
18:
David answers Saul with a wounded, reverent question: 'Wherefore doth my lord thus pursue after his servant? for what have I done? or what evil is in mine hand?' The witnesses hear in it both an appeal to innocence and an opening toward peace. The reformed Gill draws out the servant's grievance: the pursuit is both 'below' Saul 'and against his interest,' for David 'was his servant, and he would gladly have continued in his service, and done his business,' yet Saul 'drove him from it, and pursued him as a traitor, when he had not been guilty of any offence to his knowledge.'

The grammatical-historical commentators read the tone as deliberately conciliatory. Keil and Delitzsch note that after the question David 'gave him the well-meant advice to seek reconciliation for his wrath against him, and not to bring upon himself the guilt of allowing David to find his death in a foreign land,' the solemn preface 'let my lord the king hear the saying of his servant' marking what follows as weighty. Ellicott catches the visual drama: 'the conspicuous trophy of his late night raid was in his hand; we can imagine the first rays of the morning sun lighting up the glittering royal spear grasped by David,' so that 'Saul could not help recognising that at least the son of Jesse sought not his life.' The whole address, Ellicott says, 'is intensely reverent, even loving.'

The puritan Matthew Henry sets the question at the head of David's two complaints — that he was 'driven from his master and from his business,' and, more grievously, 'from his God and from his religion.' David has 'the testimony of his own conscience... that he had never done nor ever designed any mischief to the person, honour, or government of his prince,' and could point to Saul's own recent admission, 'Thou art more righteous than I' (1 Samuel 24). It was therefore 'very unreasonable and wicked' to hunt as a criminal a man who could be charged with no crime. The witnesses do not divide; they present David's protest as the plea of a clear conscience made in the very posture of restraint. The spear glittering in his hand is the answer to his own question — the surest possible evidence of what is not in it: no evil, no design, no lifted hand against the king. Innocence and mercy stand together in the one image, and Saul is left to draw the conclusion.
19:
David lays the cause of his persecution before two possibilities: 'If the Lord have stirred thee up against me, let him accept an offering; but if they be the children of men, cursed be they before the Lord; for they have driven me out... saying, Go, serve other gods.' The witnesses divide the verse between the theological puzzle of the first clause and the grief of the last. The wesleyan voices take the offering as the way of peace. Clarke reads minchah as 'a gratitude-offering': if God has stirred Saul, 'let God accept a gratitude-offering from thee, for having purged the land of a worker of iniquity'; but 'if men have, by false representations, lies, and slanders, stirred thee up against an innocent man, then let them be cursed before the Lord.' Wesley reads it as intercession: 'if the Lord hath by the evil spirit... or by his secret providence directed thy rage against me for the punishment of thine or my sins... let us offer up a sacrifice to God to appease his wrath against us.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott dwells longest on 'these difficult words.' On Wordsworth's Arabic-Targum reading the sense is, 'let me know mine offence, and I am ready to make an offering for it'; but most take it literally, 'if Jehovah has incited you... let Him smell an offering,' minchah being 'the meat offering' that signifies 'sanctification of life and devotion to the Lord.' Ellicott sets the clause within a whole biblical habit of speech, that 'the movement comes from God' — as when 'the Lord hardened Pharaoh's heart,' or 'an evil spirit from the Lord' haunts Saul — yet insists with Payne Smith that 'all leadings of Providence are to be judged by God's immutable law' and 'tested by prayer.' He reads Saul's case as the quenching of the Spirit: 'wearied or quenched, it wings its flight away from the wicked soul,' and 'the deserted heart becomes hardened, as in the case of Pharaoh; the empty shrine becomes the swept and garnished home for the evil spirit, as in the case of Saul.'

On the last clause the witnesses are one. To be driven from the land, Clarke says, was in effect to be told 'Go, serve other gods,' for it forced David 'to take refuge among idolaters.' Henry makes this David's deepest wound — not that he was driven from 'his own inheritance' but from 'the inheritance of the Lord, the holy land' — and warns that 'those that forbid our attendance on God's ordinances do what in them lies to estrange us from God, and to make us heathens'; 'we are to reckon that the greatest injury which exposes us to sin.' Wesley agrees that the exile 'exposed him to the peril of being either ensnared by their counsels, or forced by their power to worship idols.' Of such instigators David can only say, 'Cursed be they before the Lord.'
20:
David closes his plea with a cry and a pair of images: 'let not my blood fall to the earth before the face of the Lord; for the king of Israel is come out to seek a flea, as when one doth hunt a partridge in the mountains.' The witnesses read the phrase 'before the face of the Lord' two ways. The reformed Gill keeps it as it stands — God 'is omniscient, will see it, and take notice of it; and being righteous... he will avenge it' — while noting the alternative rendering, 'my blood shall not fall to the earth,' meaning David is 'continually under his eye and care.' The grammatical-historical Ellicott prefers 'far from the presence of the Lord': David dreads 'to die a violent death far away from the land which Jehovah loves.' This longing, Ellicott says, 'ever present in his heart,' is what 'made the bringing up of the Ark to a permanent sanctuary... the dream of David's life,' the 'passionate longing of the man after God's own heart to worship his Eternal Master' that breathes through his psalms.

On the two hunting figures the commentators pool their natural history. Clarke reports that the Arabs hunt partridges by repeatedly flushing them 'till at last they can knock them down with their clubs,' and 'it was in this manner that Saul hunted David.' Ellicott notes that 'to seek a flea' repeats the humbling comparison David had used once before (1 Samuel 24), and cites Erdmann's gloss on the partridge — 'the isolated from God's people... a fugitive on the mountain heights,' hunted alone 'only to kill it at all costs' — and Conder's testimony that 'the partridge is still chased on the mountains, as David was followed by the stealthy hunter Saul.'

The wesleyan Wesley draws the plea to its point: 'Remember, if thou dost it, God the judge of all men seeth it, and will avenge it; though I will not avenge myself.' And the puritan Henry weighs the disproportion the images expose — 'a poor game for the king of Israel to pursue.' David 'compares himself to a partridge, a very innocent, harmless bird,' which 'when attempts are made upon its life, flies if it can, but makes no resistance'; and Henry hears behind it the apostle's words, 'ye have killed the just, and he doth not resist you' (James 5:6). The witnesses converge on the pathos: a king with three thousand chosen men, hunting one unresisting fugitive across the hills, has made himself small in the doing, and David asks only that his innocent blood not be spilled far from the God who sees.
21:
Saul breaks: 'I have sinned; return, my son David... behold, I have played the fool, and have erred exceedingly.' The witnesses weigh the confession and divide over how much it is worth. The puritan Matthew Henry reads it warmly at first: 'this second instance of David's respect to him wrought more upon him than the former, and extorted... better acknowledgments.' Saul 'owns himself melted and quite overcome,' confesses he has 'done very wrong,' having sinned 'against God's law' and 'played the fool' against 'his own interest,' and invites David back with 'Return, my son David.' Yet Henry withholds the verdict Saul's words seem to invite: 'neither his confession nor his promise of amendment came from a principle of true repentance.' The wesleyan Wesley goes further the other way: 'nothing can be more full and ingenuous than this confession; God surely now touched his heart'; and 'it does not appear that he ever' attempted David's harm again.

Against this the reformed Gill is coldest: the confession is 'more than he acknowledged before, and yet it is to be feared he had no true sense of his sin... but, like Pharaoh, his guilty conscience for the present forced this confession from him.' Keil and Delitzsch agree that it was 'a good intention which he never carried out,' quoting the Berleburg Bible: he 'ought rather to have taken refuge with God... yea, he should have entreated David himself to pray for him,' yet 'he persists in his folly.' The grammatical-historical Ellicott hears 'a ring of falseness' both here and in Saul's blessing of David; this 'was evidently the impression made on the outlaw,' who 'silently declined the royal overtures' and 'almost immediately removed from the dominions of Saul altogether.' Clarke alone softens the word itself, suggesting 'I have sinned' may be read 'I have erred, or have been mistaken — I have taken thee to be a very different man from what I find thee to be,' though 'taken literally it was strictly true,' for Saul 'often purposed the spilling of David's blood.'

So the witnesses debate the reality of Saul's repentance. Wesley takes it for a true touch of God's grace; Gill, Henry, Ellicott and Keil take it for the momentary remorse of a hardened man, real as feeling but void of change. Ellicott marks the sombre finality the moment carries: here 'the two whom Samuel had anointed as kings — the king who has forfeited his crown, and the king of the golden future — parted for ever,' and 'never looked on each other's faces again.' Whatever the confession was worth, it was the last word of reconciliation Saul would ever speak to David.
22:
David answers by returning the trophy: 'Behold the king's spear! and let one of the young men come over and fetch it.' The two witnesses who treat the verse read the gesture as a careful refusal of even the appearance of usurpation. The reformed Gill suggests the spear may have been Saul's sceptre — 'and which David therefore would not keep, lest it should be thought or said that he had deprived him of an ensign of his royalty, and be interpreted as a token of his design to seize his crown and throne.' Having taken the spear as proof, David will not hold it as a prize; he sends it back rather than let it be read as a claim.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch record the return and the reason David gives with it: he 'bade the king send a servant to fetch back the spear and pitcher, and reminded him again of the recompense of God — Jehovah will recompense his righteousness and his faithfulness to the man into whose hand Jehovah hath given thee to-day; and I would not stretch out my hand against the anointed of the Lord.' The very act of restoring the weapon becomes the occasion for restating the principle: David had the king's life and the king's emblem in his hand, and gives both back untouched.

Between them the witnesses make the returned spear the quiet counterpart of the taken spear. To carry it off across the valley had proved how wholly Saul lay at David's mercy; to send it back proves how little David means to make of that mercy. He will not so much as retain the royal weapon overnight, lest the possession seem a reach for the throne. Where a usurper would have kept the sceptre and killed its owner, David keeps neither — he surrenders the emblem and spares the man, and lets the gesture say what his words also say: that the kingdom, when it comes, must come as God's gift and not as a thing snatched from a sleeping king. The spear that stood for royal power passes back to Saul's hand by David's own order, so that no one may ever charge that David took the crown before God gave it.
23:
David commits the whole matter to God's justice: 'The Lord render to every man his righteousness and his faithfulness; for the Lord delivered thee into my hand to-day, but I would not stretch forth mine hand against the Lord's anointed.' The reformed Gill reads the words as a confident prayer of faith. God will 'recompense every man that deals justly and faithfully with others, as he had done with Saul'; and this was 'a prayer of faith, for David doubted not that, though Saul might fail, yet God could not.' The appeal moves past the fickle king to the unfailing Judge: David asks no reward from Saul, only that the God who cannot lie will reward righteousness and faithfulness wherever they are found.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch guard the verse against a misreading. These words 'do not contain any sounding of his own praises,' as Thenius supposed, 'but are merely the testimony of a good conscience before God in the presence of an enemy, who is indeed obliged to confess his wrong-doing, but who no longer feels or acknowledges his need of forgiveness.' David is not boasting; he is witnessing — laying the plain facts of the night before God and man. And they add, looking ahead, that even Saul's coming reply, 'Blessed art thou, my son David: thou wilt undertake, and also prevail,' 'does not express any genuine goodwill toward David.'

The witnesses take the verse as the summit of David's whole defence, and as the twin of his earlier resolve to leave vengeance with God. Just as he had refused to strike Saul because 'the Lord shall smite him' in his own time, so now he refuses to claim any recompense but the Lord's. The one hand that God delivered the king into is the very hand that would not close upon him; David sets that fact before heaven not as a merit to be paraded but as a conscience to be cleared. The 'righteousness and faithfulness' he names are not a self-portrait but a standard he trusts God to honour — and, implicitly, a standard by which Saul's own pursuit stands condemned. The good conscience speaks, but it speaks to God, content to be judged and rewarded by him alone.
24:
David turns his appeal into a plea for his own preservation: 'as thy life was much set by this day in mine eyes, so let my life be much set by in the eyes of the Lord, and let him deliver me out of all tribulation.' The reformed Gill explains the force of the verb: Saul's life had been 'magnified, and made great account of' by David, 'as being the life of the king of Israel, and the Lord's anointed, and so spared.' David asks that his own life be valued as highly by God as he has valued Saul's — a prayer that his mercy be met with mercy.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the balance of the sentence and its ground: 'as thy soul has been greatly esteemed in my eyes to-day, so will my soul be greatly esteemed in the eyes of Jehovah, that He will save me out of all tribulation.' The parallel is exact and deliberate — the measure David has shown becomes the measure he asks to receive, and the whole confidence rests not on Saul's fidelity but on God's.

The puritan Matthew Henry draws out precisely this shift of trust. 'Not relying much upon Saul's promises,' David 'puts himself under God's protection and begs his favour': 'Let my life be much set by in the eyes of the Lord, how light soever thou makest of it.' Henry adds the encouraging principle: 'for his kindness to Saul, he takes God to be his paymaster, which those may with a holy confidence do that do well and suffer for it.' The witnesses do not divide; together they read the verse as David's turn from the unstable word of a king to the sure regard of God. He has just heard Saul say 'return, my son David,' and he does not believe it; so he lifts his petition over Saul's head. The logic is the gospel's own — that the mercy we show is commended to the mercy of God, who will not let the life of one who spared his enemy be lightly esteemed. David asks to be measured by the measure he has used, and rests his safety, as he had rested his vengeance and his vindication, entirely in the hands of the Lord.
25:
Saul speaks his last words to David — 'Blessed be thou, my son David: thou shalt both do great things, and also shalt still prevail' — and the two part for ever. The witnesses read the blessing as a reluctant prophecy and the parting as a settled estrangement. The reformed Gill takes the words at face value: Saul 'desired God to bless him, and pronounced him blessed himself, believing he would be a happy and prosperous man.' The grammatical-historical Ellicott hears the note of unwilling testimony: 'Saul is here again among the prophets, and foretells David's exaltation and victory,' and he sets beside it the famous cry attributed to the dying Julian, 'Vicisti Nazarene' — 'Thou hast conquered.' Keil and Delitzsch, however, deny the blessing any warmth: Saul's 'thou wilt undertake, and also prevail' 'does not express any genuine goodwill toward David.'

The wesleyan Clarke unfolds the doubled Hebrew — 'in doing thou shalt do, and being able thou shalt be able,' which the Targum renders 'in reigning thou shalt reign, and in prospering thou shalt prosper' — and admires the bearing of David through the whole scene: 'there is a vast deal of dignity in this speech of David, arising from a consciousness of his own innocence. He neither begs his life from Saul, nor offers one argument to prevail upon him... but refers the whole matter to God.' Saul is left 'speechless, except in the simple acknowledgment of his sin,' his officers silent, none daring to touch David, 'for they saw that he was most evidently under the guardian care of God, and that their master was apparently abandoned by him.' Clarke's summary is the chapter's theology in a sentence: 'How foolish are the counsels of men against God! When he undertakes to save, who can destroy?'

The puritan Matthew Henry gathers the ending. Saul 'commends and applauds David, even in the hearing of his own soldiers,' and foretells that one who 'makes conscience of doing that which is truly good may come, by the divine assistance, to do that which is truly great.' Yet the reconciliation is only 'a palliative cure': 'Saul returned to Gibeah... ashamed of the expedition he had made; but David could not take his word so far as to return with him.' The two 'parted to meet no more.' Ellicott closes the story past the grave — David never saw that face again, 'not even when the great warrior Saul lay dead,' his body burned by the men of Jabesh Gilead. The witnesses land, at the last, on Henry's pastoral verdict, and it reaches beyond the two kings to every reader: 'No reconciliation among men is firm which is not founded in and cemented by peace with God through Jesus Christ' (Ephesians 2:16). Saul's fair words could not hold, because his heart was never made new; David's safety held, because it rested wholly in the God who guards his own.
